main_summary.summary: Worth W. Long, born Worth Westmoreland Long Jr. on January 15, 1936 at Duke Hospital in Durham, North Carolina, was interviewed in Jackson, Mississippi on December 6, 2015 by Emilye Crosby for the Civil Rights History Project. The available transcript covers Long's family origins and early Durham upbringing. His father, Rev. William Worth Long Sr., was a presiding elder of the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church (AMEZ) who oversaw at least ten churches across the Durham district, traveling as a circuit rider and running quarterly conferences. His mother, Gertrude Watson Long, managed the household and the church's administrative work; she had previously worked at Liggett & Myers Tobacco Co and as a domestic for the Duke family. Long describes growing up on Formosa Avenue near North Carolina Central University, surrounded by neighbors including Henry McKinley Michaux, Dr. Albert L. Turner, and a piano studio run by C.C. Spaulding's daughter. He recalls riding his bicycle five miles to the family farm to milk cows and feed hogs, throwing newspapers at 5:30 a.m., and observing his father's hospitality to visitors including Floyd B. McKissick, Bishop Spottswood, and NAACP officials such as Alex Barnes. The transcript ends mid-sentence at 00:45:59 before reaching Long's SNCC or folklorist work.
key_themes: Family economy of farm and parsonage
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview documents the institutional ecology of mid-twentieth-century Black Durham, North Carolina — the intersection of the AME Zion Church's circuit-rider ministry, North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company, North Carolina Central University, and the Hayti commercial district. Long's account names specific figures (Floyd B. McKissick, Henry McKinley Michaux, C.C. Spaulding, Bishop Spottswood) and locates them within the residential and congregational networks that produced a generation of civil rights organizers.

The transcript also preserves first-person testimony about North Carolina's separate-but-equal evasions — including the state's decision to build a Black law school at North Carolina Central University rather than admit Floyd McKissick to the University of North Carolina, and the practice of paying Black teachers to pursue graduate degrees at Columbia and the University of Chicago rather than integrate state universities. Because the SRT is truncated at approximately 46 minutes mid-sentence, the available record covers only Long's early life and does not reach his SNCC fieldwork or his subsequent folklorist career.

chapters.title: Birth and Family Origins
chapters.summary: Long establishes his birth on January 15, 1936 at Duke Hospital in Durham, NC. He identifies his father Rev. William Worth Long Sr., born in Newell, NC (Mecklenburg County), and his mother Gertrude Watson Long, born in Chatham County, NC. Both parents had rural origins and limited formal schooling (about six grades).

chapters.title: AME Zion Circuit Ministry
chapters.summary: Long describes his father's role as a presiding elder in the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church, overseeing at least ten churches as a circuit rider. He explains the quarterly conferences, the church's missionary funding for Africa, and his mother's role as administrator handling financial aspects of the home and the district.

chapters.title: The Family Farm
chapters.summary: Long recounts the family farm five miles from Durham that supplied milk and garden food. His father drove a jeep to the farm five days a week and behind the mule. On weekends Long rode his bicycle out, milked the cow, fed the hogs and mule, and pedaled milk back to town along careful roads.

chapters.title: Father as Storyteller and Preacher
chapters.summary: Long contrasts his father's formal preaching style with his folk expressions and humor. He describes the elder Long as a shouting, stomping country preacher in the African mode. The chapter introduces the lineage of his father's first marriage to Minnie of a Chatham County free Negro family, traced through Carter G. Woodson's writings on free Negroes.

chapters.title: Mitchell's Chapel and Father's Second Marriage
chapters.summary: Long narrates his father's arrival at his first church, Mitchell's Chapel AME Zion in Pittsboro, NC, after Hood Theological Seminary and Livingstone College. After Minnie's death, his father married Gertrude Watson, a widow with three daughters whose first husband died in a mining accident in Chatham County. Gertrude's settlement allowed the family to buy a Durham duplex.
related_events: Gertrude Watson's first husband's mining accident

chapters.title: Liggett and Myers, Visiting Officials
chapters.summary: Long describes his mother's prior employment at Liggett & Myers Tobacco Co and later for the Duke family as a domestic, skills she applied to entertaining visitors. He recalls Bishop Spottswood, NAACP officials, and his mother's stepbrother Bishop E.B. Watson — who served in Liberia and brought a gold palmetto tack clip back as a gift.

chapters.title: NAACP Visitors and African Consciousness
chapters.summary: Long recalls Alex Barnes of the AME Zion Church paper bringing NAACP figures to Gertrude's table. From the living room he overheard discussions of the plight of Africans in the world and of the poor — what he characterizes as a world consciousness rooted in cultural and religious rather than narrowly political terms.

chapters.title: Formosa Avenue Community
chapters.summary: Long describes the lumber house his family inherited via mortgage from his aunt on Formosa Street, later renamed Formosa Avenue when faculty and professionals built brick and stone homes nearby for North Carolina Central University. Neighbors included Henry McKinley Michaux, Dr. Albert L. Turner of NC Mutual, and the Sharone Spaulding piano studio. Long carried manure from the family farm to his mother's garden before dawn to avoid embarrassment in the elite neighborhood.
related_events: building of NC Central University faculty homes

chapters.title: Durham Black Financial Center
chapters.summary: Long describes Durham as the financial center for Black America, anchored by North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company, North Carolina Central University, and the Durham Business School. He contrasts his own family's resources (farm and church income) with the elite professional families on Formosa Avenue who sent children to Palmer Memorial Institute and Camp Atwater in Massachusetts.

chapters.title: Hayti District and Newspaper Route
chapters.summary: Long catalogs the Black-owned businesses of the Hayti district he memorized as a paper carrier — the Bull City Drug Store, the hotel above it, the Rialto Theater and Booker T Theater, and a service printing company. He explains how counseling from customers on his collection route — the lectures and folk sayings about work ethic and success — shaped his outlook.

chapters.title: Community Uplift and Lyceum Program
chapters.summary: Long describes the practice of community uplift: as many as eight college women boarded in the Long household when the campus dormitory filled. His neighbor Dr. Holmes, a German and French professor, taught him through song — including 'O Tannenbaum' — and let him into the NC Central Lyceum program where he heard Marian Anderson, Paul Robeson, and the pianist Philippa Schuyler.

chapters.title: Floyd McKissick and Jim Crow Law School
chapters.summary: Long identifies Floyd B. McKissick — later CORE national chairman and family lawyer — as one of the first visitors at the family table. McKissick taught Long civics as a practice teacher. Long recounts how North Carolina built an entire law program at NC Central University rather than admit McKissick to the University of North Carolina law school, and how the state subsidized Black high school teachers to pursue graduate degrees at Chicago and Columbia rather than integrate.
related_events: McKissick's exclusion from UNC law school

chapters.title: First Encounters with Jim Crow
chapters.summary: Long describes accompanying his father on driving rounds to the ten district churches and an encounter at a filling station near Chapel Hill where a young white attendant did not know how to address his father — settling on 'preacher.' Long draws a lesson about self-image, dignity, and how change comes through engagement rather than theoretical conversation.
related_events: filling station encounter near Chapel Hill

chapters.title: Church as Community Center
chapters.summary: Long returns to the NAACP, describing his father's policy of establishing a chapter in every AME Zion church in the Durham district — the largest district in the denomination. He frames the church as the center of the African-American community in North Carolina, sponsoring education, economic development, and self-sufficiency, including buying mortgaged land at the courthouse to resell. The transcript truncates mid-sentence here.
related_events: NAACP chapter organizing in AMEZ churches
